title: This Teacher Built a Giant 1940s Computer With His Students — And It's Awesome!
summary:
Imagine building a giant computer from the 1940s — with your classmates! That's exactly what students at PS Academy in Arizona did.

Their teacher, Tom Burick, led them in constructing a full-scale replica of ENIAC, one of the world's first electronic computers. ENIAC was built in 1946 and was about 1,000 times faster than any other machine at the time. It was huge — it filled a whole room!

Burick is a technology instructor at a school for kids with autism and other special needs. He knows what it's like to learn differently because he has dyscalculia, a condition that makes math hard. But he says it also gives him a "superpower" — amazing 3D spatial reasoning. "I have this CAD program that runs in my head 24 hours a day," he says.

Before becoming a teacher, Burick ran a robotics company. He built a robot named the 914 PC-Bot that was like a box of Legos — you could click different parts together. His company sold about 200 robots to 17 countries. But when the 2008 financial crisis hit, the business closed.

Burick decided to become a teacher to "pay forward" the help he got as a kid. When he was young, mentors from Carnegie Mellon University and Saint Vincent College gave him textbooks and let him sit in on classes. "They really changed the trajectory of my life," he says.

Now he's inspiring his own students. The ENIAC replica project lets them use their strengths, like hyperfocus and attention to detail. Burick tells his students: "You need to have maybe just a bit more tenacity than others, but you come through with gifts and strengths."

And they built a giant computer. How cool is that?
why_it_matters: This shows that everyone has unique talents – even a condition like dyscalculia can be a superpower – and with teamwork you can do amazing things.

background_read: ENIAC was one of the first electronic computers, built in 1946 to help calculate artillery tables for the US Army.
It was enormous – it filled a whole room and used about 18,000 vacuum tubes.
Today, a small smartphone is millions of times more powerful than ENIAC.
Article_Structure: WHO: Tom Burick and his students at PS Academy in Arizona
WHAT: Built a full-scale replica of ENIAC, a 1940s computer
WHERE: PS Academy in Gilbert, Arizona
WHY: To learn about computing history and let students use their strengths like hyperfocus

perspectives.description: He wanted to give his students the same kind of support he received as a kid, and help them shine using their special abilities.
They feel proud to build something real and important, and it helps them see that their differences are strengths.
They are excited to see the school's students achieving something remarkable and gaining skills for the future.
